title: Bridge Patrol
desc: The ancient bridges need inspecting. Erosion waits for no one.

steps.text:
Bridge inspection isn't glamorous. You're handed a chisel, a jar of bonding paste, and a checklist longer than your arm. The bridges between the ridge and the falls have stood for centuries, but centuries of mist and wind take their toll.

The first three bridges check out fine — solid stone, no cracks, the Mugic-reinforced joints still humming faintly. But the fourth bridge, the narrow one over the deepest gorge, has a problem.

The center span is cracked. Not badly, but enough to see daylight through it. And the crack is growing — you can hear it tick-tick-ticking as you watch.

options.label: Get a closer look at the crack first

steps.text:
You slather paste into the crack and press hard. The paste hisses, heats up, and bonds to the stone. The ticking stops. The bridge holds.

But you used most of your paste on this one crack, and you've got six more bridges to inspect. You mark them as "deferred" on the checklist and head back.

The patrol captain looks at your report. "Deferred. Right." He doesn't seem surprised. "They're all deferred, eventually. Until they're not. Good catch on the fourth."

You lean over the crack — carefully — and peer through. Below is nothing. Just mist, and a very long way down.

But there's something else. The stone around the crack is discolored, darker than the surrounding rock. You chip at the edge with your chisel. The dark stone crumbles like chalk.

Water erosion, but from inside the bridge. A hidden channel, probably an ancient drainage line, has corroded through. You mark the spot on your checklist, apply paste to stabilize it, and note that this bridge needs a full muge inspection — the internal structure may be compromised.

The patrol captain reads your report twice. "Internal erosion. Haven't seen that in years. You may have just saved someone's life."
